analogy: Think of "Inside Our Earth" through the chapter's own opening images: It is constantly undergoing changes inside and outside. Have you ever wondered what lies in the interior of the earth? INTERIOR OF THE EARTH Just like an onion, the earth is made up of several concentric layers with one inside another (Fig. The uppermost layer... The easy idea is that the visible surface is only the outside cover. The chapter keeps asking how we can know what is inside when no one can directly reach the deep interior. So the learner must read clues: rocks, mines, drilling, volcanic material, heat, gravity, magnetism and earthquake waves. Like solving a hidden-layer puzzle, each clue tells something about the crust, mantle, core and volcanic activity without inventing anything beyond the textbook.
full_notes:
Inside Our Earth is decoded here only from the NCERT chapter text. The chapter begins by asking how the nature of the earth should be imagined and why the interior cannot be known in the same direct way as the surface. It connects the hidden interior with visible results on the surface, especially landscape development, earthquakes, tsunamis and volcanic activity.

The text explains that it is constantly undergoing changes inside and outside.

The text explains that have you ever wondered what lies in the interior of the earth?

The text explains that iNTERIOR OF THE EARTH Just like an onion, the earth is made up of several concentric layers with one inside another (Fig.

The text explains that the uppermost layer over the earth’s surface is called the crust.

The text explains that it is about 35 km. on the continental masses and only 5 km. on the ocean floors.

The text explains that the main mineral constituents of the continental mass are silica and alumina.

The text explains that it is thus called sial (si-silica and al-alumina).

The text explains that the oceanic crust mainly consists of silica and magnesium; it is therefore called sima (si-silica and ma-magnesium) (Fig.

The text explains that just beneath the crust is the mantle which extends up to a depth of 2900 km. below the crust.

The text explains that 2Inside Our Earth Do you know? • The deepest mine in the world, is in South Africa.

The text explains that in search for oil engineers have dug a hole about 6 km. deep. • To reach to the centre of the earth (which is not possible!) you will have to dig a hole 6000 km. deep on the ocean floor.

The text explains that crust Lithosphere Core-mantle boundary Mantle Outer core Inner core Fig.

The text explains that 2.1: Interior of the Earth Continental Crust Fig.

The text explains that 2.2: Continental Crust and Oceanic Crust Continent Ocean Oceanic Crust

The text explains that 8 OUR ENVIRONMENT Glossary The innermost layer is the core with a radius of about 3500 km.

The text explains that it is mainly made up of nickel and iron and is called nife (ni – nickel and fe – ferrous i.e. iron).

The text explains that the central core has very high temperature and pressure.

The text explains that rOCKS AND MINERALS The earth’s crust is made up of various types of rocks.

The text explains that any natural mass of mineral matter that makes up the earth’s crust is called a rock.

The text explains that rocks can be of different colour, size and texture.

The text explains that there are three major types of rocks: igneous rocks, sedimentary rocks and metamorphic rocks.

The text explains that when the molten magma cools, it becomes solid.

The text explains that there are two types of igneous rocks: intrusive rocks and extrusive rocks.

The text explains that can you imagine lava coming out from the volcanoes?

The text explains that lava is actually fiery red molten magma coming out from the interior of the earth on its surface.

The text explains that when this molten lava comes on the earth’s surface, it rapidly cools down and becomes solid.

The text explains that rocks formed in such a way on the crust are called extrusive igneous rocks.

The text explains that the Deccan plateau is made up of basalt rocks.

The text explains that sometimes the molten magma cools down deep inside the earth’s crust.

The text explains that solid rocks so formed are called intrusive igneous rocks.

The text explains that rocks roll down, crack, and hit each other and are broken down into small fragments.

The text explains that these loose sediments are compressed and hardened to form layers of rocks.

The text explains that these types of rocks are called sedimentary rocks.

The text explains that these rocks may also contain fossils of plants, animals and other micro- organisms that once lived on them.

The text explains that igneous and sedimentary rocks can change into metamorphic rocks under great heat and pressure (Fig.

The text explains that 2.3: Sedimentary rock turned into a Metamorphic rock Fossils: The remains of the dead plants and animals trapped in the layers of rocks are called fossils.

The text explains that do you know? • The crust forms only 1 per cent of the volume of the earth, 84 per cent consists of the mantle and 15 per cent makes the core. • The radius of the earth is 6371 km.

The text explains that iNSIDE OUR EARTH 9 Let’s do The Taj Mahal is made of white marble The Red Fort is made of red sandstone Collect pictures of some monuments and find out which are the rocks used to build them.

The text explains that the hard rocks are used for making roads, houses and buildings.

The text explains that you will be surprised to know that one type of rock changes to another type under certain conditions in a cyclic manner.

The text explains that this process of transformation of the rock from one to another is known as the rock cycle.

The text explains that you have already learnt when the molten magma cools; it solidifies to become igneous rock.

The text explains that 10 OUR ENVIRONMENT Let’s do What are the minerals found in your state?

The text explains that (iv) How are extrusive and intrusive rocks formed?

The text explains that (i) The rock which is made up of molten magma is (a) Igneous (b) Sedimentary (c) Metamorphic (ii) The innermost layer of the earth is (a) Crust (b) Core (c) Mantle (iii) Gold, petroleum and coal are examples of (a) Rocks (b) Minerals (c) Fossils (iv) Rocks which contain fossils are (a) Sedimentary rocks (b) Metamorphic rocks (c) Igneous rocks (v) The thinnest layer of the earth is (a) Crust (b) Mantle (c) Core 3.

The text explains that (i) Core (a) Earth’s surface (ii) Minerals (b) Used for roads and buildings (iii) Rocks (c) Made of silicon and alumina (iv) Clay (d) Has definite chemical composition (v) Sial (e) Innermost layer (f) Changes into slate (g) Process of transformation of the rock Rocks are made up of different minerals.

The text explains that minerals are naturally occurring substances which have certain physical properties and definite chemical composition.

The text explains that for example, coal, natural gas and petroleum.

The text explains that they are also used in industries – iron, aluminium, gold, uranium, etc, in medicine, in fertilisers, etc.

The text explains that (ii) Sedimentary rocks are formed from sediments.

The text explains that (i) What are the minerals most commonly used in the following objects?

The text explains that (ii) Identify some more objects made up of different minerals.

Source: NCERT Class 7 Our Environment, Chapter 2: Inside Our Earth

prelims_traps: Do not treat INTERIOR OF THE EARTH and 8 OUR ENVIRONMENT as identical unless the source text links them directly.
Do not add outside examples to Inside Our Earth; use only the examples and processes named in the chapter.
Do not confuse a visible surface result with the internal process or source evidence described by the text.
mains_angles: Explain Inside Our Earth through the source chain: evidence, process and visible result.
Use INTERIOR OF THE EARTH and 8 OUR ENVIRONMENT to show how the chapter connects facts with mechanisms.
Frame an answer around causes, internal working, surface expression and source limitations from the NCERT chapter.
